problem_spec.description:
Optimize the yield of a Direct Arylation Reaction (DAR) benchmark using the
vendored DAR dataset. The search space is restricted to the experimentally
observed conditions present in the CSV. During simulation, the optimizer
should not see the yield column; yields are revealed only via the dataset
oracle at the human-interrupt step.

variables.name: base_SMILES
variables.description: Base encoded as a raw SMILES string present in the dataset

variables.name: ligand_SMILES
variables.description: Ligand encoded as a raw SMILES string present in the dataset

variables.name: solvent_SMILES
variables.description: Solvent encoded as a raw SMILES string present in the dataset

variables.name: concentration
variables.description: Discrete concentration levels observed in the dataset

variables.name: temperature
variables.description: Discrete temperature levels observed in the dataset

constraints: Only propose conditions that correspond to rows present in the DAR dataset.
problem_spec.raw_description:
Optimize the yield of a Direct Arylation Reaction (DAR) benchmark using the
vendored DAR dataset. The search space is restricted to the experimentally
observed conditions present in the CSV. During simulation, the optimizer
should not see the yield column; yields are revealed only via the dataset
oracle at the human-interrupt step.

kernel_config.rationale: Switched from Matern-5/2 to Matern-3/2 based on [HYPOTHESIS:H3] which identifies multi-scale response surface with sharp 0→87% categorical transitions and smooth 79→87% continuous tuning. The campaign has experienced 5 consecutive GP model fitting failures (iterations 12-16) with Matern-5/2, suggesting the smoother kernel cannot capture the discontinuities. Matern-3/2 is more tolerant of rougher surfaces and abrupt transitions while still providing calibrated uncertainty. This is a conservative change that maintains GP interpretability while addressing the spectral mismatch identified in H3. If this fails, SMKBO would be the next escalation per H3 recommendation.

bo_config.rationale: Selected UCB over log_ei to enable explicit exploration control during reconfiguration recovery. The campaign is at iteration 16 with best yield 87.83% (iteration 9) but has suffered 5 consecutive random fallback selections due to GP failures. Current phase is exploitation but we need controlled exploration to: (a) validate the 87.83% optimum is reproducible, (b) test H1's cooperative network predictions with small perturbations, (c) explore H4's solvent polarity threshold hypothesis. Beta=2.0 balances exploration (finding reproducibility boundaries) with exploitation (returning to known high-yield region). UCB's explicit uncertainty weighting will help navigate the sharp response surface identified in [RULE:R1] where small deviations cause >25% yield variations. This is more appropriate than log_ei given the heteroscedastic noise and cliff-like transitions in the landscape.

kernel_config.rationale: Switched from Matern-5/2 to Matern-3/2 based on [HYPOTHESIS:H3] which identifies multi-scale response surface with sharp 0→87% categorical transitions and smooth 79→87% continuous tuning. The campaign has experienced 5 consecutive GP model fitting failures (iterations 12-16) with Matern-5/2, suggesting the smoother kernel cannot capture the discontinuities. Matern-3/2 is more tolerant of rougher surfaces and abrupt transitions while still providing calibrated uncertainty. This is a conservative change that maintains GP interpretability while addressing the spectral mismatch identified in H3. If this fails, SMKBO would be the next escalation per H3 recommendation.

hypotheses.text: Electron-rich dienes paired with electron-deficient dienophiles will achieve higher DAR yields due to frontier molecular orbital (FMO) matching lowering the activation barrier.
hypotheses.mechanism: FMO theory: HOMO(diene)-LUMO(dienophile) energy gap minimization accelerates the concerted [4+2] cycloaddition.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Combinations with diene HOMO energy > -6.0 eV and dienophile LUMO energy < -1.0 eV will show yields >70%.

hypotheses.text: Bulky substituents on the diene will reduce yield due to steric clash in the endo transition state, favoring exo or non-reactive conformations.
hypotheses.mechanism: Steric hindrance destabilizes the compact endo transition state required for high regio- and stereoselectivity in DAR.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Dienes with steric descriptors (e.g., Taft Es) < -1.5 will show yields <50% regardless of electronic matching.

hypotheses.text: Polar solvents (high dielectric constant) will enhance yield by stabilizing the partial charges in the asynchronous transition state of normal electron-demand DAR.
hypotheses.mechanism: Solvent stabilization of developing charges in the transition state lowers activation energy via dipole-dipole interactions.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Solvents with ε > 20 will outperform non-polar solvents (ε < 5) by >15% yield for electron-deficient dienophiles.

hypotheses.text: Elevated reaction temperatures (80-120°C) will improve yields by overcoming entropic penalties and accelerating the concerted cycloaddition without significant side reactions.
hypotheses.mechanism: DAR is concerted with negative activation entropy; higher T compensates for ΔS‡ and increases reaction rate per Eyring equation.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Temperatures 80-120°C will show 20-40% higher yields than 25-60°C for matched diene/dienophile pairs.

hypotheses.text: Lewis acid catalysts will enhance yields by coordinating to electron-withdrawing groups on the dienophile, lowering LUMO energy and accelerating the cycloaddition.
hypotheses.mechanism: Lewis acid coordination polarizes the dienophile, reducing the HOMO-LUMO gap and enabling milder reaction conditions.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Catalyzed reactions with AlCl3 or BF3·OEt2 will achieve >80% yield at lower temperatures than uncatalyzed analogs.

hypotheses.text: The DAR yield is governed by a cooperative catalytic network where XPhos-type ligands and Cs+ acetate synergistically stabilize the transition state through a combination of phosphine-metal coordination and cation-π interactions with the diene/dienophile.
hypotheses.mechanism: FMO theory with explicit solvation: The electron-rich XPhos ligand (via dicyclohexylphosphino groups) coordinates to a metal center or directly interacts with the substrate, while Cs+ (large, soft cation) facilitates ion pairing and transition state organization. The polar aprotic solvent (DMF) stabilizes the polarized transition state without competitive coordination.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Yields >80% require simultaneous presence of: (a) biaryl phosphine with ortho-dialkylphosphino substitution, (b) Cs+ or K+ with acetate (not pivalate), (c) solvent with ε > 5, (d) T ≥ 100°C. Removal of any component drops yield >15%.

hypotheses.text: The sharp yield transitions (0% to 87%) and GP model failures indicate the presence of cooperative threshold effects where multiple parameters must simultaneously meet minimum criteria for reactivity, creating a discontinuous response surface.
hypotheses.mechanism: Catalytic network theory: The DAR requires concurrent activation via ligand coordination, base-mediated deprotonation/activation, and thermal energy. When any parameter falls below threshold, the reaction fails completely (0%); above threshold, yields increase smoothly with optimization. This creates a 'cliff' in the landscape that challenges smooth GP kernels.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Regions with yield 0-5% will cluster where: ligand lacks biaryl phosphine core OR base is pivalate (any cation) OR T < 95°C OR solvent ε < 3. Transitions between 0% and >60% occur within narrow parameter ranges (<10°C, single solvent class change).

hypotheses.text: DavePhos-type ligands with electron-donating NMe2 substituents can achieve comparable performance to XPhos (>75% yields) when paired with optimal base/solvent conditions, challenging ligand specificity.
hypotheses.mechanism: Electronic tuning hypothesis: The NMe2 groups on DavePhos enhance electron density at phosphorus and may provide secondary coordination sites, partially compensating for the steric differences versus XPhos. With Cs+ acetate (not K+), DavePhos may match or exceed XPhos performance.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos-type ligand (CN(C)C1=CC=CC(N(C)C)=C1C2=CC=CC=C2P(C(C)(C)C)C3=CC=CC=C3) with Cs+ acetate/DMF/105°C/0.1M will achieve >85% yield, closing the gap to XPhos. The 9% yield penalty observed with K+ (iteration 15: 78.95% vs 87.83%) will be eliminated with Cs+.

hypotheses.text: GP model failures stem from incompatible kernel assumptions—standard RBF kernels cannot capture the sharp categorical transitions and threshold effects in this chemical space, requiring structured kernels (SMKBO) or hierarchical models.
hypotheses.mechanism: Kernel mismatch: The 51D physicochemical descriptor space creates high-dimensional sparsity, while sharp yield cliffs violate smoothness assumptions. The categorical nature of variables (ligand identity, solvent class) creates discrete jumps that continuous kernels smooth over, leading to numerical instability during hyperparameter optimization.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Switching to SMKBO with separate kernels for ligand (Tanimoto/Morgan fingerprint), solvent (dielectric constant + functional group), and continuous parameters will eliminate ModelFittingError and improve uncertainty calibration. Predictive accuracy will improve from current ~10% MAE to <5% in high-yield regions.

hypotheses.text: Lewis acid additives (AlCl3, BF3·OEt2, Zn(OTf)2) will further enhance yields beyond 87.83% by coordinating to the dienophile and lowering the LUMO energy, or by stabilizing the zwitterionic transition state.
hypotheses.mechanism: Dual activation: The current system uses thermal activation + base catalysis. Adding Lewis acid provides a third activation mode—polarization of the dienophile's electron-withdrawing groups—enabling the reaction to proceed at lower temperatures or with higher stereoselectivity.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: XPhos/Cs+ acetate/DMF/0.1M with 10 mol% AlCl3 or Zn(OTf)2 will achieve >90% yield at 90-100°C, or >95% yield at 105°C. The Lewis acid must be compatible with acetate (non-basic conditions) to avoid quenching.

hypotheses.text: The DAR yield is governed by a cooperative catalytic network where XPhos-type ligands and Cs+ acetate synergistically stabilize the transition state through a combination of phosphine-metal coordination and cation-π interactions with the diene/dienophile.
hypotheses.mechanism: FMO theory with explicit solvation: The electron-rich XPhos ligand (via dicyclohexylphosphino groups) coordinates to a metal center or directly interacts with the substrate, while Cs+ (large, soft cation) facilitates ion pairing and transition state organization. The polar aprotic solvent (DMF) stabilizes the polarized transition state without competitive coordination.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Yields >80% require simultaneous presence of: (a) biaryl phosphine with ortho-dialkylphosphino substitution, (b) Cs+ or K+ with acetate (not pivalate), (c) solvent with ε > 5, (d) T ≥ 100°C. Removal of any component drops yield >15%.

hypotheses.text: The sharp yield transitions (0% to 87%) across ligand scaffolds indicate a kinetic regime shift: XPhos-type ligands enable a low-barrier catalytic pathway that is inaccessible to other phosphines, rather than merely accelerating a common mechanism.
hypotheses.mechanism: Computational transition state theory: Non-XPhos ligands (Brettphos, DavePhos, tri-tert-butylphosphine) cannot achieve the optimal bite angle and electronic donation required to stabilize the DAR transition state, resulting in high activation barriers (>25 kcal/mol) and effectively zero yield. XPhos provides unique geometric and electronic complementarity.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DFT calculations will show ΔΔG‡ > 5 kcal/mol between XPhos and nearest alternative ligand. Experimentally, no non-XPhos ligand will exceed 30% yield under optimal conditions (Cs+ acetate, DMF, 105°C, 0.1M).

hypotheses.text: The GP model instability stems from chemical heteroscedasticity: the variance structure differs dramatically between 'active' (XPhos/Cs+ acetate/DMF) and 'inactive' (all other) regions of chemical space, violating standard GP stationarity assumptions.
hypotheses.mechanism: Statistical learning theory: The BO model assumes smooth, stationary covariance across the descriptor space. However, the chemical system exhibits sharp discontinuities—small changes in ligand SMILES cause 0%→87% yield jumps. This non-stationarity causes hyperparameter optimization to fail.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Switching to SMKBO (Structured Multi-Kernel Bayesian Optimization) with separate kernels for ligand, base, and solvent subspaces will restore model stability and reduce prediction error by >30% compared to standard GP.

hypotheses.text: The 87.83% yield optimum is robust to minor perturbations in temperature (100-110°C) and concentration (0.08-0.12M), but solvent polarity has a sharp threshold between ε~5 (butyl acetate, 86% yield) and ε~2 (p-xylene, 67% yield).
hypotheses.mechanism: Physical organic chemistry: The transition state requires sufficient solvent polarity to stabilize charge separation but is insensitive to precise polarity above a threshold. Temperature and concentration operate in a broad kinetic regime where the reaction is neither mass-transfer limited nor equilibrium-controlled.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing 100°C/0.08M/DMF and 110°C/0.12M/DMF will both yield 80-87%. Testing solvents with ε=3-4 (e.g., THF, ε=7.6→too high; anisole, ε=4.3) will show yields 70-80%, confirming the ε~5 threshold.

hypotheses.text: Residual yield variance (78-87% range) reflects uncontrolled experimental factors rather than fundamental chemical limits; the true thermodynamic yield under optimized conditions exceeds 90%.
hypotheses.mechanism: Error propagation analysis: The 7-iteration stagnation around 80-87% with diverse conditions suggests the system is near a flat optimum. However, small uncontrolled variables (oxygen, moisture, exact heating rates, reagent purity) may cause the observed variance. Alternatively, there may be a competing side reaction with ~10% selectivity that is intrinsic.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Rigorous reproduction of iteration 9 conditions with degassed solvents, inert atmosphere, and standardized heating will yield >90% or reproducibly 87±2%. If variance persists, a side product (likely oligomeric/polymeric from diene/dienophile self-reaction) is the limiting factor.

hypotheses.text: The 87.83% yield optimum is robust to minor perturbations in temperature (90-105°C) and concentration (0.057-0.1M), but solvent polarity has a threshold effect between ε~5 (butyl acetate, 86% yield) and ε~2 (p-xylene, 67% yield).
hypotheses.mechanism: Physical organic chemistry: The transition state requires sufficient solvent polarity to stabilize charge separation but is insensitive to precise polarity above a threshold. Temperature and concentration operate in a broad kinetic regime where the reaction is neither mass-transfer limited nor equilibrium-controlled. The 84.17% yield at 90°C/0.1M confirms temperature flexibility; the 79.33% at 90°C/0.057M shows concentration matters less than temperature.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing 100°C/0.08M/DMF and 110°C/0.12M/DMF will both yield 80-87%. Testing solvents with ε=3-4 (e.g., anisole, ε=4.3) will show yields 70-80%, confirming the ε~5 threshold. The optimum is a broad plateau, not a sharp peak.

hypotheses.text: The pivalate anion is fundamentally incompatible with high-yielding DAR regardless of cation, temperature, or ligand modification due to disruption of the cooperative catalytic network.
hypotheses.mechanism: Anion coordination chemistry: Pivalate (bulky, sterically hindered carboxylate) cannot effectively participate in the cation-mediated transition state stabilization required for the DAR. Unlike acetate, which can bridge between Cs+ and the substrate or form hydrogen bonds, pivalate's tert-butyl group prevents proper orientation. This is an intrinsic limitation, not optimizable by tuning other parameters.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: No combination of pivalate base with any cation (Cs+, K+, Na+, Li+), ligand (XPhos, tBuXPhos, Brettphos, etc.), temperature (90-120°C), or solvent will exceed 40% yield. The 38.48% at iteration 10 (Cs+ pivalate, 120°C) and 0.07% at iteration 16 (K+ pivalate, 105°C) represent the upper bound of pivalate performance.

hypotheses.text: The DAR yield is governed by a cooperative catalytic network where XPhos-type ligands and Cs+ acetate synergistically stabilize the transition state through a combination of phosphine-metal coordination and cation-π interactions with the diene/dienophile.
hypotheses.mechanism: FMO theory with explicit solvation: The electron-rich XPhos ligand (via dicyclohexylphosphino groups) coordinates to a metal center or directly interacts with the substrate, while Cs+ (large, soft cation) facilitates ion pairing and transition state organization. The polar aprotic solvent (DMF) stabilizes the polarized transition state without competitive coordination. Pivalate anion disrupts this network due to steric bulk and weak coordination, causing catastrophic failure regardless of ligand modification.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Yields >80% require simultaneous presence of: (a) biaryl phosphine with ortho-dialkylphosphino substitution (XPhos specifically, not tBuXPhos variants), (b) Cs+ with acetate (not pivalate, not K+), (c) solvent with ε > 5, (d) T ≥ 100°C. Removal of any component drops yield >15%.

hypotheses.text: The 87.83% yield at iteration 9 represents a kinetic regime shift where the reaction becomes mass-transport limited rather than kinetically controlled.
hypotheses.mechanism: At optimal catalyst/base/solvent conditions, the intrinsic activation barrier is sufficiently lowered that diffusion of reactants to the active site becomes rate-limiting. This explains the sharp 0→87% transitions with categorical changes (ligand/solvent identity) and the plateau behavior—once the kinetic barrier is overcome, further optimization of continuous variables (T, conc) provides diminishing returns.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Increasing stirring rate or using flow chemistry will improve yields beyond 87.83%. Alternatively, reducing catalyst loading at iteration 9 conditions will maintain high yield (>70%) if the regime is indeed mass-transport limited rather than catalyst-starved.

hypotheses.text: The GP model failure (ModelFittingError) stems from heteroscedastic noise and multi-scale response surface incompatible with single-kernel assumptions.
hypotheses.mechanism: The response surface combines: (a) sharp 0→87% categorical transitions (ligand/solvent identity changes) requiring short length scales, (b) smooth 79→87% continuous tuning (temperature/concentration) requiring long length scales, (c) heteroscedastic noise (higher variance in 'active' region). Single-kernel GPs cannot fit this spectral mixture, causing hyperparameter optimization to fail.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Switching to Spectral Mixture Kernel BO (SMKBO) or heteroscedastic GP will restore model functionality. If SMKBO succeeds, the acquisition function will propose candidates near iteration 9 conditions with small perturbations rather than random fallback selections.

hypotheses.text: The solvent effect follows a polarity threshold model rather than continuous dielectric scaling.
hypotheses.mechanism: DMF (ε~37) and butyl acetate (ε~5) both achieve >85% yields, while p-xylene (ε~2.3) drops to 67%. This suggests a critical solvation requirement: solvents must have ε > 4-5 to stabilize the polarized DAR transition state. Below this threshold, cation-anion pairing and transition state organization are insufficiently supported.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing solvents with ε = 3-5 (e.g., THF ε~7.5, ethyl acetate ε~6) will clarify the threshold. If THF/ethyl acetate achieve >75%, the threshold is confirmed around ε~5; if they fail (<50%), the threshold is higher or DMF has specific coordinating properties beyond polarity.

hypotheses.text: Lewis acid co-catalysis with the XPhos/Cs+ acetate platform can push yields beyond 87.83% by activating the dienophile.
hypotheses.mechanism: The current optimum relies on phosphine and cation effects. Adding a mild Lewis acid (e.g., Mg2+, Zn2+ salts) could further polarize the dienophile, lowering the LUMO energy and accelerating the cycloaddition. This extends the cooperative network: phosphine activates one component, Lewis acid activates the other, Cs+ maintains ionic environment.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Adding 10-20 mol% Mg(OTf)2 or Zn(OTf)2 to iteration 9 conditions will increase yield to >90%. If Lewis acids inhibit the reaction, phosphine-metal coordination is essential and cannot be disrupted.

hypotheses.text: The DAR yield is governed by a cooperative catalytic network where XPhos-type ligands and Cs+ acetate synergistically stabilize the transition state through phosphine-metal coordination and cation-π interactions.
hypotheses.mechanism: FMO theory with explicit solvation: The electron-rich XPhos ligand coordinates to a metal center or directly interacts with the substrate, while Cs+ facilitates ion pairing and transition state organization. DMF stabilizes the polarized transition state. Pivalate anion disrupts this network due to steric bulk.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Yields >80% require: (a) biaryl phosphine with ortho-dialkylphosphino substitution (XPhos specifically), (b) Cs+ with acetate, (c) solvent with ε > 5, (d) T ≥ 100°C. Removal of any component drops yield >15%.

hypotheses.text: The sharp yield transitions (0% to 87%) indicate a kinetic regime shift where XPhos-type ligands enable a low-barrier catalytic pathway inaccessible to other phosphines.
hypotheses.mechanism: Computational transition state theory: Non-XPhos ligands cannot achieve optimal bite angle and electronic donation to stabilize the DAR transition state, resulting in high activation barriers (>25 kcal/mol). XPhos provides unique geometric and electronic complementarity.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: No non-XPhos ligand will exceed 30% yield under optimal conditions (Cs+ acetate, DMF, 105°C, 0.1M). DFT will show ΔΔG‡ > 5 kcal/mol between XPhos and nearest alternative.

hypotheses.text: The GP model collapse is caused by physicochemical_descriptors failing to capture structural discontinuities between XPhos and non-XPhos ligands, creating a response surface with cliffs that Matern kernels cannot represent.
hypotheses.mechanism: Embedding deficiency: physicochemical_descriptors (molecular weight, logP, TPSA) embed XPhos and tricyclohexylphosphine similarly despite 0% vs 87% yield differences. The resulting latent space has sharp discontinuities that violate GP smoothness assumptions, causing numerical instability in kernel matrix inversion.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Switching to SMILES-based spectral mixture kernel (SMKBO) will restore model stability by learning frequency-domain representations that capture structural differences. Backtesting RMSE will drop from current ~0.5 to <0.15 within 3 iterations.

hypotheses.text: The solvent effect follows a polarity threshold model (ε > 5) rather than continuous dielectric scaling.
hypotheses.mechanism: Critical solvation requirement: DMF (ε~37) and butyl acetate (ε~5) achieve >85% yields, while p-xylene (ε~2.3) drops to 67%. Solvents must have sufficient polarity to stabilize the polarized DAR transition state and support cation-anion pairing.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing solvents with ε = 5-10 (THF, ethyl acetate) will achieve >75% if threshold is ~5. If they fail, DMF has specific coordinating properties beyond polarity.

hypotheses.text: The pivalate anion is fundamentally incompatible with high-yielding DAR regardless of cation, temperature, or ligand modification.
hypotheses.mechanism: Anion coordination chemistry: Pivalate (bulky, sterically hindered carboxylate) cannot effectively participate in cation-mediated transition state stabilization. Unlike acetate, pivalate's tert-butyl group prevents proper orientation for catalytic network formation.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: No pivalate combination with any cation, ligand, temperature, or solvent will exceed 40% yield. The 38.48% at iteration 10 and 0.07% at iteration 16 represent the upper bound.

hypotheses.text: The DAR yield is governed by a cooperative catalytic network where XPhos-type ligands and Cs+ acetate synergistically stabilize the transition state through phosphine-metal coordination and cation-π interactions.
hypotheses.mechanism: FMO theory with explicit solvation: The electron-rich XPhos ligand coordinates to a metal center or directly interacts with the substrate, while Cs+ facilitates ion pairing and transition state organization. DMF stabilizes the polarized transition state. Pivalate anion disrupts this network due to steric bulk.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Yields >80% require: (a) biaryl phosphine with ortho-dialkylphosphino substitution (XPhos specifically), (b) Cs+ with acetate, (c) solvent with ε > 5, (d) T ≥ 100°C. Removal of any component drops yield >15%.

hypotheses.text: The sharp yield transitions (0% to 87%) indicate a kinetic regime shift where XPhos-type ligands enable a low-barrier catalytic pathway inaccessible to other phosphines.
hypotheses.mechanism: Computational DFT: XPhos ligands create a sterically and electronically optimized pocket that stabilizes the rate-determining transition state by ~15 kcal/mol relative to simple phosphines. This creates a bimodal response surface with discontinuity at the ligand class boundary.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: No phosphine lacking the 2-dialkylphosphinobiphenyl scaffold will achieve >50% yield under any conditions. XPhos variants with electron-donating groups may achieve >90% yield.

hypotheses.text: The GP model failure stems from physicochemical_descriptors embedding XPhos and non-XPhos ligands similarly despite their 0% vs 87% yield difference, creating a response surface with discontinuities that Matern kernels cannot represent.
hypotheses.mechanism: Embedding analysis: Physicochemical descriptors capture global molecular properties (MW, logP, PSA) but miss the critical 2-dialkylphosphinobiphenyl scaffold topology. SMILES-based embeddings (SMKBO) would capture this structural distinction via spectral graph convolution.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Switching to SMILES-based embedding (SMKBO) will restore model stability and enable accurate prediction of the XPhos vs non-XPhos yield gap. Backtesting RMSE will improve from >0.3 to <0.15.

hypotheses.text: The DavePhos-type ligand (CN(C)C1=CC=CC(N(C)C)=C1C2=CC=CC=C2P(C(C)(C)C)C3=CC=CC=C3) shows unexpected cation sensitivity, performing better with K+ (78.95%) than Cs+ (29.31%), suggesting ligand-specific cation coordination effects.
hypotheses.mechanism: Coordination chemistry: The NMe2 groups in DavePhos may coordinate to Cs+ too strongly, sequestering the cation and preventing substrate activation. K+ has weaker coordination, preserving catalytic activity. This is ligand-specific and does not apply to XPhos.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos-type ligands will show optimal yields with K+ salts, while XPhos-type ligands show optimal yields with Cs+ salts. Mixed ligand-cation combinations will underperform.

hypotheses.text: Lewis acid additives (e.g., Mg2+, Zn2+) could enhance the DAR yield beyond 87.83% by activating the diene or dienophile component through coordination.
hypotheses.mechanism: Lewis acid catalysis: Mg2+ or Zn2+ salts coordinate to carbonyl groups, increasing dienophile electrophilicity and lowering the LUMO energy. This accelerates the cycloaddition without disrupting the XPhos/Cs+ acetate base catalytic network.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Adding 10-20 mol% Mg(OTf)2 or Zn(OTf)2 to the XPhos/Cs+ acetate/DMF/105°C/0.1M platform will increase yield to >90%. Lewis acids will not rescue non-XPhos ligand failures.

hypotheses.text: The GP model failure stems from physicochemical_descriptors embedding placing XPhos and non-XPhos ligands similarly despite their 0% vs 87% yield difference, creating a response surface with discontinuities that Matern kernels cannot represent.
hypotheses.mechanism: Embedding analysis: Physicochemical descriptors capture global molecular properties (MW, logP, PSA) but miss the critical 2-dialkylphosphinobiphenyl scaffold topology. SMILES-based embeddings (SMKBO) will capture this structural distinction via spectral graph convolution, enabling accurate modeling of the ligand-class boundary.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Switching to SMILES-based embedding (SMKBO) will restore model stability and enable accurate prediction of the XPhos vs non-XPhos yield gap. Backtesting RMSE will improve from >0.3 to <0.15. The surrogate will correctly predict >70% yield for XPhos candidates and <20% for non-XPhos candidates.

hypotheses.text: The cation effect (Cs+ > K+ by ~10-15% yield) is mediated by differential transition state stabilization through ion pairing and coordination geometry, not merely solubility or basicity.
hypotheses.mechanism: Hard-soft acid-base theory: Cs+ (large, soft cation) forms optimal ion pairs with the polarized DAR transition state without competitive coordination to the phosphine or substrate. K+ (smaller, harder) binds more tightly to acetate, reducing available cation for transition state stabilization.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Systematic cation screening (Li+, Na+, K+, Rb+, Cs+) with XPhos/acetate/DMF/105°C/0.1M will show a monotonic yield increase with cation size, plateauing at Cs+. No other cation will match Cs+ performance.

hypotheses.text: Lewis acid additives (Mg2+, Zn2+) could enhance DAR yield beyond 87.83% by activating the dienophile without disrupting the XPhos/Cs+ acetate catalytic network.
hypotheses.mechanism: Dual catalysis: Mg2+ or Zn2+ coordinates to carbonyl groups, increasing dienophile electrophilicity and lowering LUMO energy. This accelerates cycloaddition synergistically with the XPhos/Cs+ base system. Lewis acids are orthogonal to the phosphine-cation network.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Adding 10-20 mol% Mg(OTf)2 or Zn(OTf)2 to the XPhos/Cs+ acetate/DMF/105°C/0.1M platform will increase yield to >90%. Lewis acids will not rescue non-XPhos ligand failures.

hypotheses.text: The DAR yield is governed by a cooperative catalytic network where XPhos-type ligands and Cs+ acetate synergistically stabilize the transition state through phosphine-metal coordination and cation-π interactions.
hypotheses.mechanism: FMO theory with explicit solvation: The electron-rich XPhos ligand coordinates to a metal center or directly interacts with the substrate, while Cs+ facilitates ion pairing and transition state organization. DMF stabilizes the polarized transition state. Pivalate anion disrupts this network due to steric bulk.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Yields >80% require: (a) biaryl phosphine with ortho-dialkylphosphino substitution (XPhos specifically), (b) Cs+ with acetate, (c) solvent with ε > 5, (d) T ≥ 100°C. Removal of any component drops yield >15%.

hypotheses.text: DavePhos-type ligands (NMe2-substituted biaryl phosphines) require DMF specifically (ε~37) for solvation of the NMe2 groups; in non-polar solvents, intramolecular NMe2-Pd coordination poisons the catalyst, causing catastrophic yield collapse regardless of anion or cation.

In DMF, solvent molecules compete for Pd coordination, preventing NMe2 poisoning. In p-xylene (ε~2), no competitive solvation occurs, and NMe2-Pd coordination dominates, causing <10% yields. hypotheses.text: The GP model with physicochemical_descriptors embedding fails to capture the categorical structure of anion identity (acetate vs pivalate) and ligand scaffold (XPhos vs DavePhos vs simple phosphines), causing systematic proposal of known-inferior candidates despite 26 iterations of training data.
hypotheses.mechanism: Physicochemical descriptors encode molecular properties (MW, logP, TPSA) but miss structural features critical for catalysis: phosphine bite angle, biaryl torsion, anion steric bulk at the binding site. The model sees pivalate and acetate as 'similar carboxylates' and cannot learn [RULE:R1]. SMILES-based or fingerprint embeddings would capture these structural distinctions. The stored config shows matern32 but runtime uses SMKBO, causing config drift and ModelFittingError.

hypotheses.text: The XPhos scaffold enables a kinetic regime shift where the reaction becomes mass-transport limited rather than activation-controlled at concentrations above 0.08M, explaining the consistent 0.1M optimum and tolerance to solvent polarity variation.

The 83.83% yield at iteration 24 (valeronitrile) versus 87.83% (DMF) shows only ~4% solvent penalty despite ε difference of 20 units, consistent with mass-transport limitation where solvent polarity effects on activation barriers are secondary to diffusion rates.

hypotheses.mechanism: UCB acquisition with high beta prioritizes regions where the GP has high uncertainty. With physicochemical_descriptors embedding, the model has high uncertainty about pivalate vs acetate and XPhos vs triphenylphosphine because they appear similar in descriptor space. This creates a perverse incentive: the acquisition function systematically proposes candidates that are chemically distinct (high true uncertainty) but appear similar in embedding space (low model confidence). The random fallback after ModelFittingError exacerbates this by proposing truly random candidates.
